Second, who is your buyer? Is it a cultural institution or a brand budget? Third, how will your product be operated? How will the rights be realized after issuance? Fourth, where are your compliance boundaries? Have you completely avoided trading and financialization? Fifth, can you provide long-term services instead of a one-time sale? Digital collectibles are not a quick-money game. Conversely, if you truly intend to pursue this as a startup path, the aforementioned difficulties must be acknowledged and addressed one by one. Therefore, while this path may indeed be one of the few legally viable directions within China's Web3 landscape, it is not easy, nor is it suitable for those expecting instant success. You need to be more cautious and adopt a long-term perspective. You must treat it as a content and operations business requiring deep cultivation, not a market opportunity to profit from emotions and cyclical fluctuations.</div></div>

data.title: Stablecoin Freeze Case Analysis: Beware of the Threat of Human Trafficking Funds in Southeast Asia
data.description:
Illegal cross-border human trafficking in Southeast Asia is one of the most fundamental industries of local organized crime networks. Numerous industrial parks operating illegal businesses need to recruit young men from various countries and regions annually through fraud, kidnapping, and other means. This strong demand for labor has spawned a criminal industry chain combining employers, overseas labor brokers, transaction guarantee platforms, domestic labor intermediaries, and illegal payment tools.

Currently, the USD stablecoin Tether (hereinafter referred to as USDT) has been widely used in the Southeast Asian illegal cross-border human trafficking market, successfully attracting the attention of law enforcement agencies. Consequently, law enforcement agencies have begun to freeze targeted addresses or accounts involved in the case through Tether and centralized exchanges. This article aims to analyze a stablecoin freeze case to disclose this financial threat.

Analysis of the Freezing of Business Addresses of "Tianhe Global" On December 24, 2025, Tether executed an on-chain freeze on 24 addresses in one go. Bitrace intelligence shows that at least 4 of these addresses belong to Southeast Asian illegal cross-border human trafficking entities. For example, the address of the human trafficking merchant marked as "Tianhe Global" is TKkN2i3ruCis2NoGm7uNr9HeeLfaVLavT2. This established human trafficking organization primarily helps illegal industrial parks in Southeast Asia purchase employees. It has been operating for up to three years and is quite well-known in the industry. Its purchasing methods are mainly divided into three types: naked prostitution, guaranteed purchase, and secondhand purchase. As the name suggests, "naked running" refers to human trafficking activities conducted without any protective measures. Human trafficking agents located in China lure victims abroad and hand them over to overseas businesses. The businesses then directly pay the agents' addresses. Because the two parties never meet directly, there is a possibility of mutual deception. Therefore, this transaction method is extremely risky, and usually only businesses with high industry reputations support naked running transactions on a large scale. As shown in the image above, the 16,000 USDT transaction was transferred in two installments directly to the intermediary's address, and the victim was then sent to Cambodia. "Guarantee" refers to a transaction method where, in situations where the parties do not trust each other, an illegal transaction guarantee platform acts as the arbitrator. In this scenario, the merchant needs to send the agreed-upon funds to the deposit address of the illegal transaction escrow platform in advance. After the transaction is completed and both parties agree, the escrow platform then withdraws the transaction funds to the intermediary. As shown in the image above, this transaction worth 18,000 USDT was completed through the intervention of Zhencheng Guarantee, an emerging human trafficking escrow platform. The latter earned a 5% commission of 900 USDT, and the victim was sent to Cambodia. "Secondhand" refers to transactions where human trafficking companies do not purchase people directly from intermediaries, but rather from other human trafficking companies. As shown in the image above, on December 12, 2025, Tianhe International purchased one or more people from another company called Wanxiang Group for 31,527 USDT. Historical records show that there were more than one transaction between the two entities. After its core business address was frozen by Tether, Tianhe International quickly activated a new address and continued its human trafficking transactions by pledging collateral to Zhencheng Guarantee. According to business statistics of known addresses, between September 3, 2024 and January 29, 2026, after removing transactions involving internal transfers between addresses, Tianhe International outflowed at least 13.47 million USDT. This money was used to pay intermediaries for human trafficking. Even at a high price of 20,000 USDT per person, more than 600 victims were illegally trafficked by this entity. More importantly, this figure does not include internal transfers through centralized institutions or fiat currency transfers; the number of victims may be far greater than imagined. Compared to the transaction guarantee platforms and merchants at the core of organized crime networks, domestic intermediaries who directly lure victims lack sufficient financial risk control expertise. In real-world cases, many intermediaries directly use centralized trading platform deposit addresses to receive payments, resulting in human trafficking funds flowing directly into the exchanges.

Statistics on the centralized trading platform user addresses among the counterparties of Tianhe International's transactions show that OKX, Binance, and HTX received 311,415, 113,337, and 43,462 USDT respectively, accounting for 3.5% of the total outflow. ...>

data.title: Disagreements, consensus, and value redefinition in the privacy field
data.description:
Author: GD Source: X, @LaJeunesse_GD

Abstract

Amidst the fierce global competition for data sovereignty and the rapid evolution of regulatory frameworks, the privacy crypto sector is undergoing a critical period of value reshaping. As of January 14, 2026, the total market capitalization of the privacy coin sector reached $22.7 billion, with Monero (XMR) and Zcash (ZEC) together accounting for 85% of the market share.

The market exhibits significant divergence around core privacy issues: Should privacy be completely untraceable, or selectively disclosed? Should the technological path adhere to cryptographic purity, or adapt to compliance requirements? What demands drive the value of this sector?

ZEC plummeted 25% to a low of $360 on January 7 due to governance controversies, triggering an inflow of approximately $100 million into XMR. XMR subsequently reached a record high of $700 on January 14 (a weekly gain of 52.59%), becoming a direct beneficiary of the governance crisis. Beyond short-term volatility, four irreversible consensuses are emerging: selective anonymity is becoming the mainstream standard; privacy needs are a multi-faceted driving force; compliance is the path to scalability; and technological symbiosis adapts to diverse scenarios. 2026 Outlook: In the first half of the year, the sector will digest short-term emotional shocks such as the ZEC governance controversy, and fully anonymous cryptocurrencies will face continued regulatory pressure. In the second half of the year, with the ZEC governance architecture becoming clearer and ZKP accelerating its implementation in financial compliance scenarios, verifiable privacy will serve as a moat for web3 native technology, becoming a standard feature of the digital economy. The value of the privacy sector will be completely anchored to the rigid demands driven by geopolitics and the practical effectiveness of compliance implementation. 1. Introduction: The Reassessment of Privacy Value Amidst Geopolitical Shifts We are experiencing a digital territory reconstruction dominated by geopolitics. Data, as a strategic resource in the new era, has become a core target of competition and rivalry between nations. The EU's GDPR, China's data export management regulations, and the long-arm jurisdiction under the US CLOUD Act collectively outline an increasingly fragmented and adversarial global data governance landscape. In 2025, the average loss from global data breaches reached $4.4 million, with the US seeing an even higher $10.22 million (up 9% year-on-year). Malicious attacks accounted for 51% of these breaches, with the healthcare industry suffering the highest losses at $7.42 million. Against this backdrop, simple crypto assets are no longer sufficient to meet the challenges. Privacy-enhancing technologies that can safeguard data sovereignty and enable secure cross-border flows are leaping from a niche sector of cryptocurrency to a critical infrastructure supporting national security and commercial competitiveness in the digital age. In 2025, the privacy coin sector saw an overall increase of 288%, with ZEC rising 782% and XMR rising 123%, outperforming the broader market during a general correction. This chapter will delve into the profound disagreements within the privacy sector regarding definition, path, and value sources under this macro trend, and demonstrate how these disagreements collectively point to a unified and irreversible future: the value of privacy technology is being redefined by geopolitics and compliance requirements. 2. Three Core Issues of Market Disagreement The disagreement in the privacy field is not a simple battle over technical routes, but a deep-seated struggle surrounding the essence of value, implementation path, and survival logic. These disagreements are fully reflected in the market performance of two benchmark projects: Monero ($XMR) and Zcash ($ZEC). 2.1 The Essence of Privacy: Complete Anonymity vs. Selective Disclosure Core Controversy: Must privacy protection be based on complete anonymity? Or should users be allowed to make verifiable disclosures when needed? Monero represents a technological philosophy of complete anonymity. Through ring signatures, hidden addresses, and confidential transactions, $XMR achieves complete hiding of the sender, receiver, and amount of a transaction; all transactions are private and untraceable by default. Zcash, on the other hand, adopts a selective anonymity design. Through zk-SNARKs technology, users can choose to make transactions transparent or hidden, and selective disclosure is achieved through a key viewing mechanism. Users can prove transaction information to trusted parties (such as auditing firms or law enforcement agencies) without public exposure. The Two Sides of Market Feedback On the one hand, complete anonymity meets the rigid demand for combating censorship. In the first quarter of 2025, approximately 11.4% of global cryptocurrency transactions involved privacy coins, and 29% of crypto users in the Asia-Pacific region regularly use privacy coins; geopolitical instability and escalating surveillance have driven this trend. On the other hand, selective anonymity has gained wider compliance acceptance. Globally, 97 countries have implemented strict compliance frameworks for privacy coins. The EU's AMLR will officially ban exchanges from handling anonymous wallets and privacy coins on July 1, 2027, and the Dubai Financial Services Authority (DIFC) banned privacy coin trading on January 12. Against this backdrop, ZEC has 17 trading pairs on 7 major exchanges, while XMR is supported by only 5 exchanges, all of which are perpetual contracts with no spot trading pairs. Grayscale's Zcash Trust manages $151.6 million, providing a compliant channel for institutional funds to enter the privacy coin market. Although ZEC's anonymous pool accounts for only 30% of the market, its growth rate is 167%, indicating that even with choices available, user demand for privacy protection continues to grow. This optional design itself is becoming a key bridge connecting the crypto world and the traditional financial system. 2.2 Technical Path: Purity of Ring Signatures vs. Auditability of Zero-Knowledge Proofs Monero's ring signature technology obfuscates at least 10 spoofed outputs, submerging the true sender of each transaction in a web of decoys, significantly increasing the difficulty of law enforcement tracking. This design achieves complete anonymity through mathematical and network-level obfuscation, requiring no trust in any third party. Ring signatures are computationally efficient, enabling XMR to maintain stable block production with dynamically adjusted and relatively low transaction fees. Technically, XMR is advancing its FCMP++ upgrade; a community-funded optimization competition has improved the efficiency of the proof generation library by 95%, and plans are underway to add selective transparency functionality. Zcash's zk-SNARKs technology takes a different path. Zero-knowledge proofs (ZKP) allow proving a statement is true without revealing any information beyond the statement itself. In simple terms, to prove a house is yours, you don't need to bring anyone into your house; you just need to show them something they know is only in that room. This perfectly aligns with the commercial and regulatory need for data to be available but not visible. ZEC users can prove they have sufficient funds to complete transactions and are not involved in money laundering without exposing transaction history or total assets. However, ZKP proofs take longer to generate and are computationally more expensive than ring signatures, which limits the adoption of completely shielded transactions. The ZEC mainnet has completed the NU6.1 upgrade, and the roadmap includes a Tachyon scaling solution to improve scalability. The purity of the technology has resulted in regulatory resistance. XMR was delisted from Binance spot trading in February 2024, and 73 major exchanges have delisted at least one fully anonymous privacy coin. This directly cut off liquidity from mainstream fiat currency channels, limiting its value capture capabilities and liquidity. Auditability, however, opens the door to enterprise-level applications. The global ZKP market size is projected to grow from $1.28 billion in 2024 to $7.59 billion in 2033, at a CAGR of 22.1%. Application scenarios have expanded to financial anti-fraud, private stablecoin payments, HIPAA/GDPR compliant medical data sharing, anonymous voting and identity verification in government systems, and more. It is worth noting that XMR's FCMP++ upgrade plan adds outward viewing of keys, which essentially moves towards selective disclosure. ZEC's Tachyon scaling solution aims to reduce the computational cost of ZKP and improve the performance of fully shielded transactions. This convergence of technological approaches foreshadows a possible future trend of integration. 2.3 Value-Driven: Privacy Premium and Regulatory Arbitrage vs. Compliance-Driven Scale Path Core Controversy: What demands drive the value of the privacy sector? Is it legitimate privacy protection needs, or regulatory arbitrage or even illegal use? Can the market continue to expand under regulatory pressure? Cryptocurrency donations during the Russia-Ukraine war, data compliance challenges in cross-border trade, competition in digital infrastructure between nations, and the awakening of data sovereignty awareness among individuals and institutions—these factors collectively constitute a long-term, rigid demand for privacy protection: Privacy protection has evolved from a personal preference to a strategic necessity for nations, enterprises, and even individuals. Among them, Monero, due to its complete anonymity, represents the ultimate expression of privacy needs. However, this need is multifaceted and complex, with Monero being widely used in dark web transactions, as a money laundering tool for illicit activities, or as a regulatory arbitrage tool. Therefore, it inevitably faces scrutiny from global regulatory compliance: 97 countries worldwide have established strict compliance frameworks for privacy coins. The EU's AMLR will ban exchanges and custodians from handling anonymous wallets and privacy coins on July 1, 2027. The MiCA and AML package requires CASP to enforce KYC, travel rules, and crack down on coin mixers; the Dubai Financial Services Authority (DIFC) has banned privacy coins. The massive delisting wave on exchanges has directly severed the liquidity of completely anonymous coins from mainstream fiat currencies. The market is pricing in a tug-of-war. ZEC has maintained good compliance support due to its optional anonymity design, and the Grayscale Trust provides a compliant entry point for institutional funds. However, the governance crisis caused by the collective resignation of the ECC core development team on January 7 due to disagreements with the Bootstrap board over the commercialization of the Zashi wallet triggered market concerns about a project fork, causing the price to plummet 25% to a low of $360 in a single week. Although the original team established CashZ, promising to continue development and not issue new coins, and the mainnet is operating normally, GitHub activity has dropped to its lowest level since November 2021, and social media sentiment has collapsed by 90%, indicating significant short-term downward pressure. XMR's recent strong performance is mainly a direct result of the capital outflow caused by the ZEC governance crisis and the risk-averse rotation within the sector. Its long-term price support still comes from the structural demand for a privacy premium. The essence of this game lies in whether privacy premiums and regulatory arbitrage can offset the liquidity contraction brought about by global regulation; and whether the technical characteristics of complete anonymity can find a space for survival in the wave of compliance. Judging from current market performance, XMR will rely on its technological resilience to find a space for survival in DEXs and the underground market, constantly facing the long-term threat of liquidity depletion; while ZEC, although experiencing governance turmoil, retains greater possibility for future integration with TradFi due to the compliance friendliness of its selective anonymity technology. 2.4 ZEC Governance Controversy: The Boundary Between Short-Term Shocks and Long-Term Impacts The ZEC governance turmoil on January 7, 2026, provides an excellent case study for understanding the commercialization challenges of the privacy track. The entire engineering team of Electric Coin Company (ECC) resigned due to disagreements with the Bootstrap non-profit board of directors regarding the commercialization of the Zashi wallet. The team accused board members of deviating from Zcash's original privacy mission, altering employment terms to make effective work impossible, and ultimately leading to the "constructive dismissal" of figures including CEO Josh Swihart. The core conflict lay in the ECC team's desire to accelerate product launch and user growth through the commercialization of the Zashi wallet, while the Bootstrap board insisted on a non-profit structure to maintain decentralized governance. This directly caused the price of $ZEC to plummet from $503 on January 7th to a low of $360 on January 10th, a 25% drop in a single week, with a maximum daily drop exceeding 15%. However, further investigation revealed that the disagreement was limited to the commercialization architecture of the Zashi wallet and did not involve the development and iteration of the Zcash mainnet protocol layer. The Zcash Foundation explicitly stated that the network remains decentralized and functioning normally, block production and transaction settlement are all normal, and no single organization controls Zcash. CashZ, founded by the original ECC team, adopts a startup structure to accelerate innovation and commercialization, promising to continue developing the Zcash ecosystem without issuing new tokens, aiming to expand Zcash to billions of users. On-chain data shows that the size of the hidden pool, representing user demand for optional anonymity, continued to grow during the controversy (from 23% to 28-30%), indicating that the core value proposition remained unaffected. Therefore, from a long-term perspective, this turmoil should be seen as a growing pain in the project's commercialization process, rather than a failure at the technical or business model level. If governance issues are resolved and the flow of funds is clarified, ZEC's narrative as BTC insurance remains valid. 3. Unified Consensus Amidst Divergence: Four Irreversible Trends Despite intense debate in the market regarding the definition, technological path, and value source of privacy, a deeper analysis of the underlying logic reveals four irreversible consensuses forming, collectively reshaping the value foundation of the privacy sector. 3.1 Consensus 1: Selective Anonymity Becomes the Core and Industry Standard of Privacy Whether from the perspective of regulatory tolerance or the feasibility of commercial applications, the narrative of complete anonymity is giving way to controlled anonymity. Firstly, there is the unification of regulatory compliance. Data regulations in major global jurisdictions do not support complete anonymity, but leave room for controlled disclosure. The EU's GDPR requires data minimization, while MiCA requires KYC/AML verification. These two requirements seem contradictory, but ZKP offers a solution by "proving the facts without exposing the data." China's "Measures for the Authentication of Personal Information Export" allows for cross-border data flow where "data is usable but not visible." The US GENIUS Act and the pending CLARITY Act both strengthen regulatory oversight, indirectly driving demand for compliant privacy. Market choices are also showing clear signals: ZEC, despite governance controversies, still holds a 30% market share in the privacy coin market, and its selective anonymity design is key to maintaining its mainstream position. The contrasting data is striking: • **Exchange Support:** ZEC has 17 trading pairs on 7 exchanges, while XMR is supported by only 5 exchanges, all of which are perpetual contracts; • **Institutional Funding Access:** Grayscale Zcash Trust manages $151.6 million (a 228% increase by 2025) and provides access for compliant institutions, while XMR has no similar product; • User adoption trends: ZEC's covert pool increased from 11% to 30%, covering 70% of total transactions, while XMR suffered a 51% attack in September 2025. The fundamental shift lies in the value moving from concealment to control: "Verifiability when disclosure is needed, and concealment when protection is needed" has become the greatest common denominator in balancing innovation and regulation, and connecting the crypto world and the traditional world. This shift gives auditable privacy technologies like ZKP a structural advantage. 3.2 Consensus Two: Privacy Needs are a Multi-faceted Driver, and Their Role as an Auxiliary Channel in Fund Rotation is Emerging. Privacy protection needs are multi-layered and persistent, including legitimate privacy claims, regulatory arbitrage motives, and the objectively existing use by black and gray market industries. In other words, the value of the privacy sector comes from this combination of diverse needs, rather than a single attribution. The multi-faceted structure of these needs mainly comes from the following points: 1. Legitimate Privacy Needs: Personal financial privacy protection, cross-border corporate payments, financial anti-fraud, medical data sharing, etc. 2. Regulatory arbitrage demand: Tax planning, cross-border wealth allocation, capital control circumvention, DeFi, etc.; 3. Illegal demand from the black and gray markets: Dark web transactions, money laundering, ransomware, etc.; 4. Others: Speculation based on supply scarcity, HODL (Hope for Die) among tech enthusiasts, etc. Additionally, a noteworthy phenomenon is that in this market round, privacy coins have played a role as a conduit for funds entering and leaving the crypto market in specific scenarios. With stricter capital controls and increased KYC requirements, some funds choose privacy coins as a transit point for anonymous deposits, meaning funds first enter ZEC. Anonymization is achieved through hidden pools, followed by exiting to transparent addresses or cross-chain bridges to prevent address tracking. Currently, this primarily serves the deposit and withdrawal needs of regulatory arbitrage and illicit activities, rather than the overall market demand. Mainstream deposit and withdrawal channels still rely on BTC/ETH spot ETFs, direct stablecoin exchanges, and compliant exchange fiat currency channels. Market performance confirms this; in 2025, the privacy coin sector saw an overall increase of 288%, a performance that cannot be explained solely by illicit activity or simply attributed to geopolitical hedging or regulatory arbitrage. A more reasonable explanation is the resonance of diverse privacy needs. Privacy will become a key narrative this year, but the core logic has shifted from "resisting state surveillance" to the commercialization and compliance of data autonomy. Simply dividing privacy needs into "just geopolitical risk aversion" or "evil tools for crime" fails to explain the complex realities of the market. **Privacy technology itself is value-neutral; its value is driven by society's multi-layered needs for privacy: from completely legal to gray areas to explicitly illegal, forming a continuous spectrum.** The market differentiation between XMR and ZEC essentially lies in their different positions within this spectrum: the former serves the entire spectrum, including the needs of more black and gray market users; the latter focuses on compliance, excluding illegal activities while retaining privacy protection in compliant scenarios through selective disclosure. 3.3 Consensus Three: Compliance is the Only Path to Mainstream Applications and Large-Scale Funding. The wave of delistings from exchanges has clearly drawn a red line. If the privacy sector wants to shed its niche, marginal, or even illegal labels, it must proactively embrace compliance. This is not a compromise, but a prerequisite for large-scale survival and development. While XMR faces continued pressure from regulators, ZEC maintains good exchange support and institutional funding channels due to its selective anonymity: in addition to Grayscale's Zcash Trust providing a compliant entry point for institutions, there's also the DAT exposure established by Cypherpunk Technologies; ZKP technology is rapidly penetrating enterprise-level applications, including financial anti-fraud, the privacy stablecoin zkUSD, medical data sharing, and government voting and identity verification. All of this proves one point: only compliance can open the doors to enterprise-level applications, government cooperation, and traditional capital, enabling the leap from niche tools to infrastructure. Emerging privacy projects in 2026, such as Zama (FHE fully homomorphic encryption) and Railgun (EVM), are expected to emerge. Privacy), Aztec (ZK-rollup), and Arcium (Solana confidential computing) all employ programmable, selectively disclosed designs to build a "Privacy 2.0" ecosystem. This also confirms a trend: **future mainstream privacy products will inevitably be compliance-friendly products.** 3.4 Consensus Four: Technological Symbiosis, Differentiated Adaptation to Diverse Scenarios The debate over the merits of ZKP versus ring signatures is gradually giving way to a more rational understanding: **no single technology can be universally applicable.** ZKP will dominate compliant scenarios, such as finance, data trading, government affairs, and healthcare, where integration with existing legal and commercial systems is necessary. Ring signatures, on the other hand, will retain their use in anti-censorship scenarios, such as small payments, cross-border remittances, and black market activities where anti-censorship is paramount. While XMR faces delisting from exchanges, it remains viable on DEXs and P2P platforms. The market remains active. Meanwhile, we also see technological evolution towards convergence: XMR's FCMP++ upgrade plan adds the "outward viewing of keys" function, essentially moving towards selective disclosure; ZEC's Tachyon scaling solution aims to reduce ZKP computational costs and improve the performance of fully shielded transactions; emerging hybrid technology stacks, such as FHE, privacy smart contracts, and ZK-rollups, all aim to provide modular solutions for different risk appetites. The future landscape is more likely to see the coexistence of multiple technologies, forming a layered ecosystem based on different risk appetites and application needs. Full anonymity technology retains value in specific scenarios, while auditable privacy technology becomes mainstream infrastructure; the two are complementary rather than substitutive. 4. 2026 Trend Outlook: From Game Theory to Value Reshaping Through Convergence Based on an in-depth analysis of disagreements and consensus, combined with on-chain data, derivatives markets, institutional reports, and social narratives, I make the following judgments on the development trajectory of the privacy sector in 2026: 4.1 2026 H1: Increased Differentiation, Sentiment Digestion and Liquidity Restructuring under Regulatory Pressure XMR's Survival Challenges XMR, representing fully anonymous cryptocurrencies, will continue to face substantial pressure from global regulation.
